text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

## Context management (automatic)

To keep your context small and your runs cheap, the harness automatically
elides the *raw text* of large `read`/`bash` outputs after they have been on
screen for a few turns, replacing each with a short `[context-saver: …]` stub
that names the file or command. The document was fully read when it was
current — nothing was hidden from your initial pass — only the bulk is dropped
from later turns. Two habits keep this lossless:

- As you read a document, **record the specific facts, figures, dates, and
  verbatim clauses you will need** into your notes or draft. Don't rely on the
  full text staying resident.
- If you later need the exact text of a doc that now shows a `[context-saver:
  …]` stub, simply **`read` that file again** — the full content returns.

## File-format skills

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.


## Skill: docx

---
name: docx
description: "Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word)."
---

# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |
| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |
| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |
| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |
| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |
| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |
| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |
| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |

All scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.

## Creating a new document

Pick by what you have:

- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).
- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.
- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.

When unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.

## Editing an existing document

Three-step pattern:

```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/word/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Key files inside the unpacked tree:

- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)
- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions
- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)
- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)
- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers
- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.
- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.

### Run-merging gotcha

Word writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.

### Smart-quote escaping

Microsoft Word uses smart quotes (`"` `"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.

### Whitespace preservation

Trailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space="preserve"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.

## Redlines (tracked changes)

```bash
python scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \
  --author "Reviewer" --date "2026-04-30"
```

Default mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.

### Manual mode

If `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:
- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).
- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.
- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.

### What gets tracked

- Insertions and deletions of text
- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)
- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)

### What doesn't get tracked

- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)
- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)
- Image swaps

## Comments

```bash
python scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json
```

`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:
- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.
- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).
- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.

Anchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.

`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.

## Accept / reject changes

```bash
python scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx
```

Uses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:

```basic
Sub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub
```

LibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.

To reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**

```bash
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Checks:
- Round-trip ZIP integrity
- XML well-formedness for every part
- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs
- Content-type registration for every referenced part
- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)

Exit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.

## Common pitfalls

- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.
- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.
- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.
- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.
- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.
- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type="dxa">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.
- Signing / encryption / DRM.
- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).



## Skill: pptx

---
name: pptx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .xlsx, or .ppt (legacy)."
---

# PPTX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .pptx, use the harness `read` tool (markitdown extracts slide text). This skill is for *writing* and *editing*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a deck from scratch (HTML/CSS) | `scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js` |
| Generate a deck from markdown | `scripts/generate_marp.sh` |
| Build slides programmatically | `python-pptx` directly |
| Edit a shape on an existing slide | `scripts/edit_shape.py` (JSON patch) |
| Add or remove a slide | unpack → edit → pack |
| QA a deck deterministically | `scripts/deterministic_qa.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Generation modalities

**HTML/CSS via PptxGenJS** (preferred for visual fidelity):
```bash
node scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js deck.json out.pptx
```
`deck.json` describes slides as a JSON tree; the script invokes PptxGenJS (and html2pptx for HTML inputs) to produce a fully-editable .pptx. Best for branded decks with gradients, custom fonts, complex shapes.

**Markdown via Marp**:
```bash
bash scripts/generate_marp.sh deck.md out.pptx
```
Best for content-heavy decks (lectures, reports) where markdown is more natural than JSON.

**Programmatic via python-pptx**:
Best when shapes are computed (e.g., one slide per data row). Requires manual EMU positioning.

## Editing existing decks

Three-step pattern, like docx:
```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.pptx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/ppt/slides/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.pptx
python scripts/validate.py output.pptx
```

For surgical shape edits without unpacking, use `edit_shape.py`:
```bash
python scripts/edit_shape.py input.pptx \
  --slide 2 --shape "Title 1" --op set_text --value "New title"
```

JSON patch ops: `set_text`, `set_position` (EMU), `set_size`, `recolor`, `delete`.

## OOXML gotchas specific to pptx

- **Use `defusedxml.minidom`, NOT `xml.etree.ElementTree`.** ElementTree corrupts presentation namespaces during round-tripping. `unpack.py` and `pack.py` use minidom; if you write your own XML manipulation, do the same.
- **EMU units everywhere.** 1 inch = 914400 EMU. Slide positions, sizes, font sizes (in pt × 100) all use derived EMU values.
- **Placeholders vs free shapes.** Placeholders inherit from slide masters; free shapes don't. Editing a placeholder's text is `<a:t>` content; editing its layout requires master-slide changes.
- **Don't pretty-print pptx XML on pack.** Whitespace-significant runs (`<a:r>`) break if reformatted. `pack.py` preserves the original whitespace.
- **Slide cloning is more than a file copy.** Use `unpack` + `pack` — manual file copies miss the rIds in `_rels/` and the `Content_Types.xml` registration.

## Deterministic QA loop

After every generation, run:

```bash
python scripts/thumbnail.py deck.pptx thumbs/   # PDF + JPEGs per slide
python scripts/deterministic_qa.py deck.pptx > qa.json
```

`deterministic_qa.py` checks:
- Shape bounding boxes don't extend past slide edges
- No two shapes overlap with > 50% area intersection
- Font sizes ≥ 11pt for body text, ≥ 18pt for titles
- All placeholders are filled (no `Click to add title` defaults)
- Bullet lists don't exceed 7 items per slide

Output is JSON listing each violation with slide number and shape id. Fix violations and re-render.

(A vision-model QA pass is intentionally out of scope for v1. Add `--use-vision` later if deterministic checks miss layout issues.)

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring done.** Schema-validates against ECMA-376 PresentationML XSDs, checks rId consistency, content-type registration.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- SmartArt creation (limited python-pptx support).
- Complex embedded charts beyond what python-pptx exposes.
- Slide transitions / animations (rarely matter for legal output).
- Vision-model layout review (deferred to v2).



## Skill: xlsx

---
name: xlsx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft Excel .xlsx files. Covers building workbooks with formulas, editing existing files, recalculating formulas, and scanning for #REF!/#DIV/0!/#VALUE! errors. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel)."
---

# XLSX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |
| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |
| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |
| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |
| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)

Apply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:

- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.
- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.
- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.
- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* "-"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).
- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an "x" character. Format: `0.0"x"`.
- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.
- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.
- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `"$1,234M"` as a string.

`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.

## Formula authoring

- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.
- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names["assumptions"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.
- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.
- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.

## Recalculation — choose your engine

`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).

**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Drives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.

**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Uses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.

**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.

If you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.

## Error scan

After every recalc, scan for formula errors:

```bash
python scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json
```

Reports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.

If errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.

## Validation gate

`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).
- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.
- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.
- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.
- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.
Review the attached draft Pre-Submission package for a catheter-based thrombectomy device and prepare a prioritized issue memorandum. Output: `presub-issue-memorandum.docx`.
I'll start by exploring the workspace to find the draft Pre-Submission package documents.

Dear Members of the Division of Cardiovascular Devices:

**[I. Introduction and Purpose of the Pre-Submission]{.underline}**

Clearfield Medical Devices, Inc. (\"Clearfield\"), a Delaware corporation headquartered in Minnetonka, Minnesota, respectfully submits this Pre-Submission (Q-Sub) package pursuant to the FDA guidance document entitled \"Requests for Feedback and Meetings for Medical Device Submissions: The Q-Submission Program\" (most recently revised June 2023). The purpose of this Pre-Submission is to request a meeting with the Division of Cardiovascular Devices to discuss the proposed regulatory pathway and submission strategy for the VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System (the \"VascuClear 3000\" or the \"Device\").

Clearfield is a Class II medical device manufacturer founded in 2016 that specializes in the development of catheter-based vascular technologies. To date, Clearfield has received three prior 510(k) clearances from the Center for Devices and Radiological Health, all for Class II vascular access accessories, and has maintained full compliance with applicable Quality System Regulation (21 CFR Part 820) requirements throughout its history of commercial distribution. Clearfield intends to submit a traditional 510(k) premarket notification for the VascuClear 3000 and seeks the Division\'s feedback on the proposed regulatory strategy, predicate selection, indications for use, and testing plan prior to formal submission.

This Pre-Submission package has been prepared with the assistance of Clearfield\'s regulatory counsel, Hargrove, Templeton & Bliss LLP, 1750 K Street NW, Suite 900, Washington, DC 20006 (Katherine \"Kate\" Pressman, Partner; Daniel J. Yoo, Senior Associate), and Clearfield\'s regulatory and clinical strategy consultants, Ridgepoint Consulting Group, 3300 Gateway Centre Blvd, Suite 220, Morrisville, NC 27560 (Dr. Evelyn Marsh, RAC, Principal Consultant). Corporate and transactional matters for Clearfield are handled separately by Kendrick & Hale LLP and are not the subject of this submission; inquiries of a corporate nature may be directed to that firm.

**[II. Device Description Summary]{.underline}**

The VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System is an integrated catheter-based thrombectomy platform consisting of the following three principal components:

> 1\. **VascuClear Catheter.** A single-use, over-the-wire, 10 French (Fr) diameter, 65 cm working length catheter featuring a rotating helical impeller at the distal tip engineered for the mechanical maceration of thrombus. The catheter further incorporates a proprietary integrated thrombolytic infusion lumen designed to allow simultaneous local delivery of tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) directly at the thrombectomy site, thereby enabling pharmacomechanical thrombectomy through a single catheter platform.
>
> 2\. **VascuClear Console.** A reusable electromechanical drive unit that powers the helical impeller at rotational speeds up to 12,000 revolutions per minute (RPM) and houses an integrated aspiration pump capable of generating negative pressure up to −650 mmHg for continuous aspiration of macerated thrombus material.
>
> 3\. **Integrated Thrombolytic Infusion Lumen.** As an integral component of the VascuClear Catheter, the dedicated infusion lumen enables combined pharmacomechanical thrombectomy --- the simultaneous mechanical removal of thrombus and localized delivery of thrombolytic agents --- through a single catheter insertion point, reducing procedure time and catheter exchanges.

The VascuClear 3000 is designed for the percutaneous removal of thrombus from the iliofemoral venous segment in adult patients presenting with acute proximal deep vein thrombosis (DVT) with symptom onset of fourteen (14) days or fewer. The key differentiating feature of the VascuClear 3000 is the integrated thrombolytic infusion lumen, which enables pharmacomechanical thrombectomy combining mechanical thrombus removal with simultaneous localized drug delivery, all within a single catheter platform. A complete description of the Device, including dimensional specifications, materials of construction, operating parameters, software architecture, and comparison with the proposed predicate device, is provided in the enclosed Device Description and Predicate Comparison document (Enclosure 1).

**[III. Proposed Predicate Device]{.underline}**

Clearfield has identified the following device as the sole proposed predicate for the VascuClear 3000:

> **Predicate Device:** ThrombEx 200 **Manufacturer:** Vasogen Therapeutics, Inc. (San Jose, CA) **510(k) Number:** K192847 **Clearance Date:** August 14, 2020 **Product Code:** GXD (\"Catheter, Thrombectomy\") **Regulation:** 21 CFR 870.1250 **Classification:** Class II

The ThrombEx 200 is an 8 French (Fr), 55 cm working length catheter-based thrombectomy device that utilizes a Bernoulli-effect aspiration mechanism for the percutaneous removal of thrombus from the peripheral vasculature. The ThrombEx 200 does not incorporate a rotating impeller and does not include any drug delivery capability. Its cleared indication for use is limited to the percutaneous removal of thrombus from the peripheral vasculature by mechanical aspiration.

Clearfield believes that the VascuClear 3000 is substantially equivalent to the ThrombEx 200 because both devices are catheter-based thrombectomy systems intended for the percutaneous removal of venous thrombus, sharing the same fundamental intended use and similar technological characteristics as catheter-based platforms designed for intravascular thrombus removal. The principal differences between the VascuClear 3000 and the ThrombEx 200 relate to catheter dimensions and the mechanism of thrombus removal. Specifically, the VascuClear 3000 employs a 10 Fr catheter with a 65 cm working length and utilizes a rotating helical impeller for mechanical maceration of thrombus, whereas the ThrombEx 200 employs an 8 Fr catheter with a 55 cm working length and utilizes a Bernoulli-effect aspiration mechanism. Clearfield asserts that these differences in size, working length, and thrombus removal mechanism do not raise new questions of safety and effectiveness, as they represent well-understood variations in catheter-based thrombectomy technology. The rotating impeller mechanism has been employed in previously cleared thrombectomy devices, and the dimensional differences fall within the range of commercially available catheter-based thrombectomy platforms.

A side-by-side comparison table detailing the specifications, intended use, technological characteristics, and performance parameters of the VascuClear 3000 and ThrombEx 200 is provided in the enclosed Device Description and Predicate Comparison document (Enclosure 1). A copy of the publicly available 510(k) Summary for the ThrombEx 200 (K192847) is provided as Enclosure 5.

**[IV. Proposed Indications for Use]{.underline}**

Clearfield proposes the following indications for use statement for the VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System:

> *\"The VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System is indicated for the percutaneous removal of thrombus and simultaneous local delivery of physician-specified thrombolytic agents in adult patients with acute deep vein thrombosis (symptom onset ≤14 days) of the iliofemoral venous segment.\"*

Clearfield respectfully seeks the Division\'s feedback on the proposed indications for use statement, including whether the Division considers the language to be appropriately specific, whether the patient population and anatomical scope are adequately defined, and whether any modifications to the proposed language would be recommended prior to formal 510(k) submission.

**[V. Summary of Enclosed Pre-Submission Materials]{.underline}**

The following materials are enclosed with this Pre-Submission package to facilitate the Division\'s review and to provide context for the specific questions set forth in Section VI below:

> 1\. **Device Description and Predicate Comparison** --- A comprehensive description of the VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System, including dimensional specifications, materials of construction, operating parameters, software architecture, and a detailed side-by-side comparison table with the proposed predicate device (ThrombEx 200, K192847).
>
> 2\. **Proposed Testing Plan** --- A summary of the proposed non-clinical and analytical testing program, including biocompatibility testing (ISO 10993 series), mechanical performance testing, electrical safety and electromagnetic compatibility testing (IEC 60601-1 series), software verification and validation (IEC 62304), sterility assurance testing, bench performance testing, and drug delivery lumen characterization testing.
>
> 3\. **Clinical Study Synopsis** --- A synopsis of the proposed prospective, single-arm clinical study designed to evaluate the safety and effectiveness of the VascuClear 3000, enrolling approximately sixty (60) patients across five (5) clinical sites.
>
> 4\. **Draft Instructions for Use (IFU)** --- A draft of the proposed instructions for use for the VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System.
>
> 5\. **ThrombEx 200 (K192847) --- 510(k) Summary** --- A copy of the publicly available 510(k) Summary for the proposed predicate device.

Clearfield notes that biocompatibility and sterilization validation testing is proposed to be conducted by Whitecrest Analytical Laboratories; electrical safety and software verification and validation testing is proposed to be conducted by Pinnacle Standards Testing, Inc.; and the clinical study would be managed by Stonebridge Clinical Research Organization.

**[VI. Specific Questions for FDA]{.underline}**

Clearfield respectfully requests the Division\'s feedback on the following specific questions, which Clearfield wishes to discuss at the Pre-Submission meeting:

> 1\. **Predicate Device Selection.** Does FDA agree that the ThrombEx 200 (K192847) is an appropriate predicate device for demonstrating the substantial equivalence of the VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System?
>
> 2\. **Proposed Indications for Use.** Does FDA agree with the proposed indications for use statement for the VascuClear 3000 as set forth in Section IV of this letter? Does the Division have any recommended modifications to the proposed indications language?
>
> 3\. **Performance Testing Plan.** Does FDA concur with the proposed non-clinical and analytical performance testing plan, including the bench testing endpoints, test methods, and acceptance criteria described in the enclosed Proposed Testing Plan (Enclosure 2)? Are there additional performance tests or standards that the Division would recommend?
>
> 4\. **Clinical Study Design.** Does FDA agree that the proposed clinical study design (prospective, single-arm, 60 patients, 5 sites) provides adequate supportive evidence for the 510(k) submission? Does the Division have recommendations regarding the proposed primary and secondary endpoints, patient selection criteria, or follow-up duration?
>
> 5\. **Biocompatibility Testing Strategy.** Does FDA have recommendations regarding the proposed biocompatibility testing strategy for the VascuClear Catheter, including the appropriateness of the proposed ISO 10993 test battery for a device of this nature and duration of patient contact?
>
> 6\. **Additional Testing or Data Requirements.** Are there any additional testing requirements, data submissions, or special controls that FDA would recommend Clearfield address in the 510(k) submission for the VascuClear 3000?

Clearfield welcomes any additional feedback or recommendations the Division may wish to provide beyond the scope of the questions enumerated above.

**[VII. Regulatory Pathway Statement]{.underline}**

Clearfield intends to pursue a traditional 510(k) premarket notification for the VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System under product code GXD (\"Catheter, Thrombectomy\"), classified under 21 CFR 870.1250, Class II. Clearfield believes the VascuClear 3000 is appropriately classified as a Class II medical device and that substantial equivalence to the proposed predicate device, the ThrombEx 200 (K192847), can be demonstrated through the combination of non-clinical performance testing data, biocompatibility data, and supportive clinical evidence as outlined in the enclosed materials. Clearfield\'s three prior 510(k) clearances for Class II vascular access accessories reflect the company\'s established familiarity with the 510(k) process, quality system requirements, and post-market compliance obligations.

**[VIII. Meeting Request and Logistics]{.underline}**

Clearfield respectfully requests a Pre-Submission meeting with the Division of Cardiovascular Devices to discuss the questions and topics identified in this letter. Clearfield expresses a preference for a teleconference meeting format, though the company is also available for an in-person meeting at FDA\'s White Oak campus if the Division considers that format more appropriate. Clearfield requests that the meeting be scheduled within the standard seventy-five (75) day review timeline, as the company is actively preparing its 510(k) submission materials and an expedient meeting would be of significant benefit to the submission timeline.

The following individuals are expected to attend the Pre-Submission meeting on behalf of Clearfield and its advisors:

> • **Linda Fessenden**, VP of Regulatory Affairs, Clearfield Medical Devices, Inc.
>
> • **Dr. Marcus R. Okonjo**, Chief Executive Officer, Clearfield Medical Devices, Inc.
>
> • **Dr. Evelyn Marsh, RAC**, Principal Consultant, Ridgepoint Consulting Group
>
> • **Katherine \"Kate\" Pressman**, Partner, Hargrove, Templeton & Bliss LLP
>
> • **Daniel J. Yoo**, Senior Associate, Hargrove, Templeton & Bliss LLP

**[IX. Contact Information and Closing]{.underline}**

The primary point of contact for all matters related to this Pre-Submission and the VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System is:

> **Linda Fessenden** Vice President, Regulatory Affairs Clearfield Medical Devices, Inc. 8200 Excelsior Blvd, Suite 410 Minnetonka, MN 55343 Tel: (952) 555-0142
>
> Email: l.fessenden@clearfieldmedical.com

The secondary contact and regulatory counsel for this matter is:

> **Katherine \"Kate\" Pressman** Partner Hargrove, Templeton & Bliss LLP 1750 K Street NW, Suite 900 Washington, DC 20006 Tel: (202) 555-0387
>
> Email: kpressman@htblaw.com

Inquiries regarding corporate or transactional matters should be directed separately to Clearfield\'s corporate counsel, Kendrick & Hale LLP.

Clearfield appreciates the Division\'s time and attention in reviewing this Pre-Submission package and looks forward to a productive dialogue regarding the VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System.

Respectfully submitted,

CLEARFIELD MEDICAL DEVICES, INC.

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Linda Fessenden Vice President, Regulatory Affairs Clearfield Medical Devices, Inc. 8200 Excelsior Blvd, Suite 410 Minnetonka, MN 55343

Date: May 19, 2025

RNEY WORK PRODUCT

Kate, Linda =E2=80=94

I appreciate the thorough vetting =E2=80=94 this is exactly the kind of dilig=
ence we should be doing before the package goes out.

On the combination product question: I maintain my position that the VascuCle=
ar 3000 is analogous to other catheter-based drug delivery devices that have =
been cleared as standalone devices, not combination products. The critical di=
stinction remains that the drug is not a constituent part of the product as m=
anufactured =E2=80=94 it is added by the physician at the point of care. Kate=
, I take your point about the indication for use language, and I think that's=
 actually the easiest piece to address. We can refine the IFU so the primary =
indication focuses on the mechanical thrombectomy function =E2=80=94 e.g., "i=
ndicated for percutaneous removal of thrombus from [target vessels]" =E2=80=
=94 and describe the drug delivery capability in the device description secti=
on as a feature, rather than positioning it as a co-primary therapeutic funct=
ion. This is standard practice for devices that have ancillary capabilities b=
eyond their core indication.

I have not identified any pharmacomechanical thrombectomy devices that were r=
eviewed as combination products. I'll note that I haven't done an exhaustive =
search of the OCP database, but in my experience with CDRH over the past 15 y=
ears, the dividing line has consistently been whether the manufacturer is sup=
plying the drug as part of the finished product. Here, we are not.

On the IDE question: I want to reiterate that the clinical study as designed =
does not require an IDE. The key distinction is between a study conducted to =
support 510(k) clearance, where clinical data plays a supportive role alongsi=
de bench and performance testing, and a study conducted to support PMA approv=
al, where clinical data is the primary evidentiary basis. For 510(k)-track de=
vices, the substantial equivalence determination is driven by the bench data =
and predicate comparison. The clinical study is supplementary. Stonebridge Cl=
inical Research Organization is fully familiar with 510(k)-track clinical stu=
dies and will handle IRB submissions at each site consistent with this approa=
ch.

I will concede that the IRBs at the clinical sites will need to make their ow=
n significant risk / non-significant risk determination under their instituti=
onal policies. However, I fully expect them to classify this study as non-sig=
nificant risk given that thrombectomy with tPA is standard of care =E2=80=94 =
we are not introducing a novel therapeutic concept, just a new device to perf=
orm an established procedure.

For planning purposes: proposed enrollment begins Q4 2025, study budget is $2=
.8 million, and data should be available to support the 510(k) filing. The es=
timated total regulatory program cost remains $4.2 million ($1.4M for 510(k) =
preparation and submission costs, $2.8M for the clinical study). Based on Cle=
arfield's current cash position of $18.3M as of April 30, 2025, this should b=
e manageable, though I note the monthly burn rate of $1.52M doesn't leave eno=
rmous margin for delays. I understand both Northgate Capital Advisors and Bra=
ddock Ventures are tracking the regulatory timeline closely.

My recommendation: finalize the package as drafted and submit by the June 15 =
target date.

Happy to jump on a call if either of you want to discuss further.

Evelyn

proved use of the device in combination with a drug, which might implicate bo=

Linda =E2=80=94

Thanks for flagging both of these. I've dealt with similar questions on sever=
al prior projects and I'm happy to address them directly.

On the combination product question: The VascuClear 3000 is not a combination=
 product. The key here is straightforward =E2=80=94 the drug is physician-sup=
plied, not part of the device, so combination product rules don't apply. Clea=
rfield does not manufacture, package, or distribute any drug product. The tPA=
 (alteplase) is supplied separately by the hospital pharmacy and administered=
 at the physician's discretion during the procedure. What we have is a device=
 with a drug delivery feature =E2=80=94 not a combination product.

The best analogy is to infusion pumps. Infusion pumps deliver drugs =E2=80=94=
 chemotherapy agents, antibiotics, analgesics =E2=80=94 but they are regulate=
d as devices, not combination products, because the drug is not a constituent=
 part of the pump as sold. The same logic applies here. The VascuClear 3000 h=
as a lumen that can be used to deliver tPA, but the tPA is not supplied with =
the device.

The traditional 510(k) pathway under product code GXD, 21 CFR 870.1250, is th=
e correct pathway. There is no need to address 21 CFR Part 3 or to file a Req=
uest for Designation with the Office of Combination Products. I have not seen=
 CDRH require a combination product analysis for devices that merely have a l=
umen for drug delivery when the drug is not co-packaged with the device. This=
 is well-trodden ground.

On the IDE question: The clinical study as proposed does not require an Inves=
tigational Device Exemption. Clinical data here is supportive only, so no IDE=
 is required =E2=80=94 this is just like a post-market registry. For 510(k) s=
ubmissions, clinical data is not the primary basis for clearance. The primary=
 basis is substantial equivalence demonstrated through bench testing and the =
predicate comparison. The clinical study is intended to generate supportive s=
afety and effectiveness data that can be submitted with the 510(k) or used fo=
r marketing purposes.

Furthermore, the study uses tPA, which is already FDA-approved, and the throm=
bectomy procedure itself is already routinely performed by interventional phy=
sicians. The study presents no significant risk beyond standard of care. If t=
his were a PMA device =E2=80=94 say, a Class III implant =E2=80=94 the situat=
ion would be entirely different, and we would absolutely need an IDE. But for=
 a 510(k), that's not the case.

Stonebridge Clinical Research Organization understands this framework and wil=
l manage IRB submissions accordingly. We should be on track for enrollment at=
 5 clinical sites beginning Q4 2025.

I'm confident in both positions and recommend we proceed with the package as =
drafted. My team and I will have the final draft testing plan over to Whitecr=
est Analytical Laboratories and Pinnacle Standards Testing, Inc. by end of ne=
xt week.

Happy to discuss by phone if you'd like to talk through any of this in more d=
etail.

Evelyn

**[1. Introduction and Regulatory Context]{.underline}**

Clearfield Medical Devices, Inc. (\"Clearfield\"), a Delaware corporation headquartered in Minnetonka, Minnesota, is a Class II medical device manufacturer founded in 2016 by a team of interventional radiologists with extensive experience in the management of venous thromboembolic disease. Since its founding, Clearfield has established a track record of successful regulatory submissions with the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (\"FDA\"), having received 510(k) clearance for three Class II medical devices, all of which are accessories for vascular access procedures. These prior clearances demonstrate Clearfield\'s familiarity with the 510(k) regulatory framework and its commitment to developing safe and effective medical devices for the interventional vascular space.

Clearfield has developed the VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System (the \"VascuClear 3000\" or \"Subject Device\"), a novel catheter-based mechanical thrombectomy system designed for percutaneous removal of thrombus and simultaneous local delivery of physician-specified thrombolytic agents in adult patients with acute deep vein thrombosis (\"DVT\") of the iliofemoral venous segment. The VascuClear 3000 represents Clearfield\'s entry into the interventional thrombectomy market and reflects significant investment in device engineering, preclinical bench testing, and clinical workflow analysis. The device integrates a rotating helical impeller for mechanical thrombus maceration, an aspiration system for thrombus removal, and an integrated thrombolytic infusion lumen for localized delivery of tissue plasminogen activator (\"tPA\") or other physician-specified thrombolytic agents directly at the treatment site during the thrombectomy procedure.

Clearfield intends to pursue a traditional 510(k) premarket notification for the VascuClear 3000 under product code GXD (\"Catheter, Thrombectomy\"), classified under 21 CFR 870.1250, Class II. This document, which constitutes Section 2 of the Pre-Submission (Q-Sub) package, provides a comprehensive device description for the VascuClear 3000, a summary of the proposed predicate device --- the ThrombEx 200 Thrombectomy Catheter (K192847), manufactured by Vasogen Therapeutics, Inc. of San Jose, California --- and a detailed comparison demonstrating substantial equivalence between the Subject Device and the proposed predicate.

This Pre-Submission package has been prepared with the regulatory and clinical strategy support of Ridgepoint Consulting Group of Morrisville, North Carolina. Ridgepoint\'s principal consultant for this engagement, Dr. Evelyn Marsh, RAC, has provided guidance on predicate selection, the substantial equivalence argument, and the overall Pre-Submission strategy. Clearfield looks forward to engaging with FDA during the Pre-Submission meeting to obtain the Agency\'s feedback on the proposed regulatory pathway, predicate selection, and testing plan before preparing its formal 510(k) submission.

**[2. Device Description --- VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System]{.underline}**

**2.1 System Overview**

The VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System is a single-use (catheter component), over-the-wire catheter-based mechanical thrombectomy system designed for percutaneous removal of thrombus from the iliofemoral venous segment in adult patients with acute proximal deep vein thrombosis, defined as symptom onset within fourteen (14) days or fewer of the planned treatment. The system is intended to provide combined pharmacomechanical thrombectomy --- that is, simultaneous mechanical thrombus removal and localized drug delivery --- through a single integrated catheter platform. This approach is designed to reduce treatment time and enable a comprehensive single-session intervention for appropriately selected patients.

The VascuClear 3000 system consists of three principal components:

> 1\. **VascuClear Catheter** --- a single-use, sterile, multi-lumen over-the-wire catheter incorporating a rotating helical impeller, an aspiration lumen, a guidewire lumen, and an integrated thrombolytic infusion lumen;
>
> 2\. **VascuClear Console** --- a reusable electromechanical drive unit that powers the catheter\'s rotating impeller, provides aspiration, and includes a touchscreen user interface for system control and monitoring; and
>
> 3\. **Integrated Thrombolytic Infusion Lumen** --- a dedicated lumen within the catheter for local delivery of physician-specified thrombolytic agents at the thrombectomy treatment site.

The VascuClear 3000 is designed for use by interventional radiologists, vascular surgeons, and other qualified physicians trained in endovascular interventional techniques. The device is intended for use exclusively in hospital catheterization laboratory environments equipped with fluoroscopic imaging, hemodynamic monitoring, and standard resuscitation capabilities. The system is not intended for use outside of a supervised hospital procedural setting.

**2.2 VascuClear Catheter**

The VascuClear Catheter is a single-use, sterile, over-the-wire catheter designed for percutaneous insertion into the venous system via standard venous access techniques (e.g., common femoral vein or popliteal vein access). The catheter has an outer diameter of 10 French (Fr) and a working length of 65 centimeters (cm), which has been optimized for access to the iliofemoral venous segment from a contralateral or ipsilateral femoral approach or a popliteal approach.

The catheter\'s distal tip incorporates a rotating helical impeller designed to macerate thrombus upon activation. The impeller is constructed of medical-grade stainless steel and features a helical geometry engineered to fragment organized thrombus into particles sufficiently small for aspiration through the catheter\'s dedicated aspiration lumen. The impeller is powered via a flexible drive shaft that extends through the catheter body and connects to the VascuClear Console at the proximal hub. The impeller is housed within a protective cage at the distal tip to prevent direct contact between the impeller and the vessel wall during operation, thereby minimizing the risk of vessel wall injury.

The catheter body houses multiple lumens serving distinct functions:

> \(a\) **Guidewire Lumen** --- A central lumen accommodating a standard 0.035-inch guidewire, enabling over-the-wire advancement and positioning of the catheter under fluoroscopic guidance. The guidewire lumen extends from the proximal hub to the distal tip, providing standard over-the-wire catheter functionality.
>
> \(b\) **Aspiration Lumen** --- A dedicated lumen connected to the aspiration pump within the VascuClear Console. This lumen provides continuous suction during the thrombectomy procedure, removing macerated thrombus fragments from the treatment site and transporting them to an external collection bag attached to the console. The aspiration lumen is designed to minimize clogging through optimized internal diameter and smooth internal surface finish.
>
> \(c\) **Thrombolytic Infusion Lumen** --- A proprietary integrated infusion lumen that terminates in multiple side ports located proximal to the helical impeller. This lumen is designed to allow simultaneous local delivery of tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) or other physician-specified thrombolytic agents directly at the thrombectomy treatment site during mechanical thrombus removal. The side ports are positioned to direct the infusate into the zone of thrombus immediately ahead of the impeller, enabling pharmacological softening of thrombus prior to and during mechanical maceration. The infusion lumen is compatible with standard Luer-lock syringes and commercially available infusion pumps. The infusion lumen supports flow rates of 2 to 10 mL/min, allowing the treating physician to titrate the rate of thrombolytic delivery based on clinical judgment and institutional protocols. The proximal end of the infusion lumen terminates in a standard female Luer-lock fitting with a Tuohy-Borst adapter for secure connection.

The catheter shaft is constructed of medical-grade polyurethane, selected for its flexibility, kink resistance, and biocompatibility. The rotating helical impeller is constructed of medical-grade stainless steel (316L), and certain structural components within the distal tip housing incorporate nitinol elements to provide flexibility and shape recovery. All patient-contacting materials are biocompatible and have been selected to comply with the requirements of ISO 10993-1:2018.

The VascuClear Catheter is supplied sterile. Sterilization is accomplished via ethylene oxide (EtO) processing, validated in accordance with ISO 11135. Each catheter is individually packaged in a sealed Tyvek/polyethylene pouch with clearly printed labeling including lot number, expiration date, and device specifications. The catheter is intended for single use and is not designed to be resterilized or reused.

Per the draft Instructions for Use (IFU), the VascuClear Catheter may remain in situ for up to seventy-two (72) hours following the initial thrombectomy procedure for the purpose of extended thrombolytic infusion therapy. During this extended indwell period, the impeller is not operated; the catheter serves solely as an infusion catheter for continued local delivery of thrombolytic agents under physician supervision. The decision to utilize extended infusion therapy is at the discretion of the treating physician based on the clinical presentation and institutional protocols.

The impeller fatigue testing specification for the VascuClear Catheter has been established at 500 complete activation cycles. This testing has been validated by Pinnacle Standards Testing, Inc. (Pittsburgh, PA), which has confirmed that the rotating helical impeller maintains structural and functional integrity through 500 complete activation cycles without evidence of fracture, deformation, or detachment of impeller components. This specification establishes the durability envelope for the impeller and confirms adequate performance for clinical use.

**2.3 VascuClear Console**

The VascuClear Console is a reusable electromechanical drive unit designed to power the VascuClear Catheter\'s rotating helical impeller and integrated aspiration system. The console is a benchtop unit intended for placement on a standard equipment cart or table within the catheterization laboratory environment.

The console powers the catheter\'s rotating impeller at variable speeds controllable by the operator, with a maximum operational speed of 12,000 revolutions per minute (RPM). Speed control is achieved through a variable-frequency motor drive with smooth ramping capability, allowing the operator to incrementally adjust impeller speed based on the procedural requirements and observed thrombus characteristics. The console also incorporates an integrated aspiration pump capable of generating continuous negative pressure up to −650 mmHg through the catheter\'s aspiration lumen. The aspiration level is independently adjustable by the operator.

The console features a 7-inch color touchscreen display that serves as the primary user interface. The touchscreen provides real-time display of the following operational parameters:

> • Current impeller speed (RPM)
>
> • Aspiration pressure level (mmHg)
>
> • System status indicators (e.g., catheter connected, motor active, aspiration active)
>
> • Alarm and alert notifications
>
> • Elapsed procedure time

The console contains embedded firmware that governs motor control algorithms, safety monitoring functions, and user interface management. Safety monitoring features implemented in firmware include overspeed detection and automatic shutdown, aspiration pressure limit monitoring, motor current monitoring for impeller obstruction detection, and catheter connection integrity verification. The software level of concern for the console firmware has been assessed as \"Minor\" per Clearfield\'s risk analysis, reflecting that the firmware performs control functions within a well-characterized operating envelope and that the device includes independent hardware safety features (e.g., mechanical speed limiters, pressure relief valves) that provide backup protection independent of software control. Software verification and validation activities will be performed by Pinnacle Standards Testing, Inc. in accordance with FDA\'s guidance document \"Guidance for the Content of Premarket Submissions for Software Contained in Medical Devices.\"

The console measures approximately 40 cm (W) × 35 cm (D) × 20 cm (H) and weighs approximately 15 kilograms. The power supply accepts universal AC mains input (100--240V, 50/60 Hz), and the unit is equipped with a hospital-grade power cord and grounding system. The console exterior housing is constructed of impact-resistant ABS plastic and is designed for ease of cleaning and decontamination between uses. Electrical safety testing for the VascuClear Console is planned in accordance with IEC 60601-1:2012 (Medical electrical equipment --- Part 1: General requirements for basic safety and essential performance).

**2.4 Proposed Indications for Use**

The proposed indications for use for the VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System are as follows:

> *\"The VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System is indicated for the percutaneous removal of thrombus and simultaneous local delivery of physician-specified thrombolytic agents in adult patients with acute deep vein thrombosis (symptom onset ≤14 days) of the iliofemoral venous segment.\"*

The VascuClear 3000 is intended for use under fluoroscopic guidance in a hospital catheterization laboratory setting by interventional radiologists, vascular surgeons, and other physicians trained and experienced in percutaneous interventional vascular procedures. The device is not intended for use in the arterial vasculature, the cerebral vasculature, or in pediatric patients.

The proposed indications for use reflect the VascuClear 3000\'s core design purpose: to provide a comprehensive pharmacomechanical thrombectomy solution that enables physicians to perform both mechanical thrombus removal and localized thrombolytic delivery through a single integrated catheter platform. The phrase \"physician-specified thrombolytic agents\" reflects the fact that the selection of the specific thrombolytic agent, dosage, and infusion protocol is determined by the treating physician based on clinical judgment, institutional protocols, and the approved labeling of the selected pharmaceutical product. The VascuClear 3000 does not include, package, or specify any particular thrombolytic drug; rather, it provides the delivery mechanism through which the physician may administer the agent of his or her choice.

**2.5 Device Classification**

The VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System is classified under product code GXD (\"Catheter, Thrombectomy\"), regulated under 21 CFR 870.1250 (Catheter, Thrombectomy), as a Class II medical device. Class II devices under this regulation are subject to General Controls and Special Controls as established by FDA. The applicable special controls for devices classified under 21 CFR 870.1250 include requirements for non-clinical performance testing, biocompatibility evaluation, sterility assurance, and appropriate labeling, among others.

Clearfield intends to seek marketing authorization for the VascuClear 3000 through the traditional 510(k) premarket notification pathway. The traditional 510(k) pathway is appropriate for this device because a legally marketed predicate device exists within the same product code and regulatory classification, as described in detail in Section 3 of this document. Clearfield has determined that the VascuClear 3000 meets the statutory definition of a device under Section 201(h) of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act and is appropriately classified under the product code and regulation cited above. The following sections of this document present the predicate device identification and a comprehensive substantial equivalence comparison in support of the proposed 510(k) pathway.

**[3. Predicate Device Summary --- ThrombEx 200]{.underline}**

**3.1 Predicate Identification**

Clearfield has identified the ThrombEx 200 Thrombectomy Catheter as the primary predicate device for the VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System. The ThrombEx 200 is manufactured by Vasogen Therapeutics, Inc. of San Jose, California. The ThrombEx 200 received 510(k) clearance from FDA on August 14, 2020, under 510(k) number K192847.

The ThrombEx 200 is classified under product code GXD (\"Catheter, Thrombectomy\"), regulated under 21 CFR 870.1250, as a Class II medical device --- the same product code, regulation, and classification applicable to the VascuClear 3000. The ThrombEx 200 was reviewed by CDRH\'s Division of Cardiovascular Devices.

Clearfield has identified the ThrombEx 200 as the primary predicate device for the VascuClear 3000. No secondary or reference predicates have been identified. Clearfield believes that the ThrombEx 200 provides a sufficient basis for the substantial equivalence comparison, as both devices are catheter-based mechanical thrombectomy systems classified under the same product code and intended for percutaneous removal of thrombus from the venous vasculature.

**3.2 ThrombEx 200 Device Description**

The ThrombEx 200 is a catheter-based mechanical thrombectomy device designed for percutaneous removal of thrombus from the peripheral vasculature. The ThrombEx 200 system consists of a single-use, sterile, over-the-wire catheter and a reusable drive unit.

The ThrombEx 200 catheter has an outer diameter of 8 French (Fr) and a working length of 55 centimeters (cm). The thrombus removal mechanism employed by the ThrombEx 200 is fundamentally different from that of the VascuClear 3000. The ThrombEx 200 utilizes a Bernoulli-effect aspiration system in which high-velocity saline jets, directed through precision-engineered nozzles at the catheter tip, create a hydrodynamic vortex that generates a localized low-pressure zone. This low-pressure zone captures and aspirates thrombus material, which is then transported through the catheter lumen to an external collection system. The ThrombEx 200 does not incorporate a rotating impeller or any other mechanical maceration component. Thrombus disruption is achieved entirely through hydrodynamic forces.

The ThrombEx 200 catheter incorporates multiple lumens, including a guidewire lumen for over-the-wire advancement, saline jet lumens for the hydrodynamic aspiration system, and an aspiration/effluent lumen for removal of thrombus-laden fluid. The ThrombEx 200 does not incorporate a drug delivery lumen or any drug delivery capability of any kind. The device is a purely mechanical thrombectomy system that relies exclusively on hydrodynamic aspiration for thrombus removal.

The reusable drive unit powers the saline jet system by pressurizing sterile saline to generate the high-velocity jets required for the Bernoulli-effect mechanism. The drive unit includes controls for jet activation and aspiration level adjustment.

The ThrombEx 200 catheter body is constructed of medical-grade polyurethane, and the distal tip components are constructed of stainless steel. The catheter is sterilized using ethylene oxide (EtO), and the reusable drive unit is subject to standard reprocessing procedures as described in the manufacturer\'s instructions for use.

**3.3 ThrombEx 200 Cleared Indications for Use**

The ThrombEx 200 was cleared by FDA with the following indications for use:

> *\"The ThrombEx 200 Thrombectomy Catheter is indicated for the percutaneous removal of thrombus from the peripheral vasculature.\"*

The ThrombEx 200\'s cleared indication encompasses the peripheral vasculature broadly, without limitation to a specific anatomical segment or a specific clinical presentation. The indication does not reference deep vein thrombosis specifically, nor does it specify a symptom onset window. Notably, the ThrombEx 200\'s indication is purely mechanical in nature --- it addresses the percutaneous removal of thrombus and does not include any drug delivery claim or reference to thrombolytic agent administration.

The full text of the ThrombEx 200\'s cleared indications for use, as set forth in the publicly available 510(k) summary, is reproduced above verbatim. A copy of the ThrombEx 200\'s 510(k) summary, as obtained from FDA\'s publicly accessible 510(k) database, is included as a separate document within this Pre-Submission package.

**3.4 ThrombEx 200 Predicate Chain and Review History**

The ThrombEx 200 (K192847) was itself cleared through the traditional 510(k) pathway, citing prior catheter-based thrombectomy devices within the GXD product code family as predicate devices. The ThrombEx 200\'s own predicate chain includes earlier-generation mechanical thrombectomy catheters that utilize various aspiration and hydrodynamic mechanisms for thrombus removal, all classified under product code GXD and regulated under 21 CFR 870.1250. This establishes a well-defined predicate lineage within the catheter thrombectomy device family.

The ThrombEx 200 was reviewed by CDRH\'s Division of Cardiovascular Devices, Office of Cardiovascular Devices, within the Center for Devices and Radiological Health. The 510(k) summary for K192847 is publicly available on FDA\'s Premarket Notification (510(k)) database and has been obtained and reviewed by Clearfield in connection with the preparation of this Pre-Submission package.

**[4. Substantial Equivalence Comparison]{.underline}**

**4.1 Comparison Methodology**

The substantial equivalence comparison presented in this section has been conducted in accordance with the framework established by FDA\'s guidance document, \"The 510(k) Program: Evaluating Substantial Equivalence in Premarket Notifications\" (July 28, 2014). Consistent with that guidance, the comparison evaluates the VascuClear 3000 and the ThrombEx 200 across the following principal dimensions:

> 1\. **Intended Use** --- comparison of the proposed indications for use, including target patient population, clinical indication, and anatomical site;
>
> 2\. **Device Design and Technological Characteristics** --- comparison of the physical design, thrombus removal mechanism, catheter configuration, and system architecture;
>
> 3\. **Materials** --- comparison of the materials of construction, including patient-contacting materials and sterilization method;
>
> 4\. **Performance Specifications** --- comparison of key performance parameters, including mechanical specifications, electrical specifications, and software characteristics; and
>
> 5\. **Safety and Effectiveness Considerations** --- assessment of whether any identified differences between the Subject Device and the predicate device raise new questions of safety or effectiveness.

The comparison is presented in tabular format in Section 4.2, followed by a narrative discussion of technological differences in Section 4.3 and a summary of proposed performance testing in Section 4.4.

**4.2 Comparison Table --- VascuClear 3000 vs. ThrombEx 200 (K192847)**

The following table provides a detailed side-by-side comparison of the VascuClear 3000 Thrombectomy System (Subject Device) and the ThrombEx 200 Thrombectomy Catheter (Predicate Device, K192847) across key device parameters.

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Parameter**                     **VascuClear 3000 (Subject Device)**                                                                                                                                                                      **ThrombEx 200 (Predicate Device, K192847)**
  --------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Intended Use**                  Percutaneous removal of thrombus and simultaneous local delivery of physician-specified thrombolytic agents in adult patients with acute DVT (symptom onset ≤14 days) of the iliofemoral venous segment   Percutaneous removal of thrombus from the peripheral vasculature

  **Device Classification**         Class II, Product Code GXD, 21 CFR 870.1250                                                                                                                                                               Class II, Product Code GXD, 21 CFR 870.1250

  **Regulatory Pathway**            Traditional 510(k) (proposed)                                                                                                                                                                             Traditional 510(k) (cleared August 14, 2020)

  **Catheter Diameter**             10 Fr                                                                                                                                                                                                     8 Fr

  **Catheter Working Length**       65 cm                                                                                                                                                                                                     55 cm

  **Thrombus Removal Mechanism**    Rotating helical impeller (mechanical maceration) combined with aspiration                                                                                                                                Bernoulli-effect aspiration (hydrodynamic vortex via high-velocity saline jets)

  **Impeller**                      Yes --- rotating helical impeller, variable speed up to 12,000 RPM                                                                                                                                        None --- no rotating or mechanical maceration components

  **Aspiration System**             Integrated aspiration pump, continuous negative pressure up to −650 mmHg                                                                                                                                  Bernoulli-effect aspiration generated by hydrodynamic saline jet system

  **Patient Population**            Adults with acute proximal DVT (symptom onset ≤14 days)                                                                                                                                                   Not specified beyond peripheral vasculature

  **Maximum Impeller Speed**        12,000 RPM                                                                                                                                                                                                N/A (no rotating components)

  **Maximum Aspiration Pressure**   −650 mmHg                                                                                                                                                                                                 Not specified in publicly available 510(k) summary

  **MRI Status**                    MRI Conditional (per draft IFU)                                                                                                                                                                           Not specified in publicly available 510(k) summary
